title: Geopolitical Position and Maritime Geography
data_note: Statistics compiled from publicly available sources. Web verification was unavailable at compilation time; URLs point to known authoritative publication pages. Users should verify current figures at listed sources.

statistics.metric: Taiwan Strait width at narrowest point (Pingtan to Hsinchu)
statistics.context: The Taiwan Strait is approximately 180 km wide on average, narrowing to ~130 km between Pingtan Island (Fujian) and Hsinchu. The strait is approximately 350 km long.
statistics.verification_note: Standard geographic measurement; consistent across NGA, NOAA, and academic sources

source.name: NOAA National Centers for Environmental Information / GEBCO Bathymetric Data

statistics.context: Average depth is approximately 60 meters, with the deepest point in the Penghu Channel reaching ~100 meters. The shallow strait makes submarine operations difficult and ASW operations relatively easier.
statistics.verification_note: Consistent with GEBCO 2023 global bathymetric grid and published naval analyses

source.name: International Hydrographic Organization / NGA Sailing Directions Pub. 161

statistics.context: The Bashi Channel lies between Taiwan's southernmost point and the Batanes Islands of the Philippines, spanning approximately 250 km. It is the primary deep-water passage between the South China Sea and the Philippine Sea / Western Pacific.
statistics.verification_note: Standard geographic measurement from nautical charts

statistics.context: The Bashi Channel reaches depths exceeding 4,500 meters in places, making it a strategically critical deep-water transit route for submarines. PLAN submarines must transit this channel to access the open Pacific.

statistics.metric: Annual commercial shipping transits through Taiwan Strait

source.name: Bloomberg / Marine Traffic AIS data analysis

statistics.context: Approximately 88,000 commercial vessels transit the Taiwan Strait annually, representing nearly half of global container fleet movements. The strait is one of the world's busiest shipping lanes.
statistics.verification_note: Widely cited figure from AIS vessel tracking data; Bloomberg 2022 analysis updated by subsequent reporting

statistics.metric: Share of global trade transiting Taiwan Strait and surrounding waters
statistics.unit: percent of global maritime trade value
statistics.context: Approximately 21% of global trade by value passes through the South China Sea and Taiwan Strait corridor. Any disruption would affect global supply chains worth trillions of dollars.
statistics.verification_note: Cross-referenced with CSIS ChinaPower estimates and UNCTAD data

statistics.metric: Annual LNG shipments transiting South China Sea and Taiwan Strait
statistics.context: Roughly one-third of global LNG trade transits the South China Sea. Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan are the primary destination markets and would be severely affected by any disruption.
statistics.verification_note: EIA South China Sea analysis regularly updated; figure consistent across 2022-2024 reporting

statistics.metric: PLA Navy (PLAN) vessels in total fleet

source.name: U.S. Department of Defense, Military and Security Developments Involving the PRC 2024

statistics.context: The PLAN is the world's largest navy by number of vessels. The fleet includes aircraft carriers, destroyers, frigates, corvettes, submarines, and amphibious ships. The DoD report notes the PLAN had approximately 370 battle force ships by end of 2024.
statistics.verification_note: From annual DoD China Military Power Report to Congress (2024 edition)

statistics.metric: PLAN incursions into Taiwan ADIZ (2024 full year)

source.name: Taiwan Ministry of National Defense / Gerald C. Brown ADIZ tracker

statistics.context: Taiwan's MND began publicly reporting PLA aircraft incursions into its ADIZ in September 2020. The number of detected sorties has increased substantially year-over-year, from ~380 in 2020, ~960 in 2021, ~1,727 in 2022, ~1,709 in 2023, reaching similar or higher levels in 2024.
statistics.verification_note: Based on MND daily press releases compiled by independent trackers; figures are approximate due to varying reporting methodologies

statistics.metric: PLAN naval transits through First Island Chain chokepoints (annual)

source.name: Japan Ministry of Defense Joint Staff Press Releases

statistics.context: Japan's MOD regularly tracks and publishes PLAN naval transits through chokepoints including the Miyako Strait, Bashi Channel, and Tsushima Strait. PLAN task groups have conducted increasingly frequent 'far seas' training exercises in the Western Pacific. Estimate of ~140 transits annually based on compiled MOD reports from 2023-2024.
statistics.verification_note: Compiled from Japan MOD Joint Staff press releases; exact annual tally varies by source and counting methodology

statistics.metric: First Island Chain total length (Kuril Islands to Borneo)

source.name: U.S. Naval War College / CSIS Asia Maritime Transparency Initiative

statistics.context: The First Island Chain extends approximately 5,600 km from the Kuril Islands through Japan, the Ryukyu Islands, Taiwan, the Philippines, to Borneo. Taiwan sits at the geographic center and is the critical link in the chain.
statistics.verification_note: Standard geographic measurement from published strategic studies

source.name: Sea Around Us / Flanders Marine Institute VLIZ Maritime Boundaries

statistics.context: Taiwan's EEZ is relatively modest compared to its strategic importance. The claimed zone is approximately 83,000 sq km, though overlapping claims with Japan (Senkaku/Diaoyu area) and the Philippines complicate the picture.
statistics.verification_note: EEZ area varies by source due to disputed boundaries; figure from Sea Around Us project
